title: Viral Misinformation on TikTok Drives Dangerous Surge in Neurological Self-Diagnosis
description: A new study reveals that up to 52% of social media posts about ADHD and autism contain harmful misinformation, prompting unwarranted self-diagnoses among young people.
lede: Rampant misinformation on social media platforms is driving a surge in young people incorrectly self-diagnosing with neurological conditions like ADHD and autism. Researchers warn that platforms like TikTok and YouTube are fueling misunderstandings, leading to delayed real diagnoses and pathologizing normal behavior.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Inaccurate social media posts about attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and autism are directly linked to a rise in young people falsely believing they have neurodevelopmental conditions. Experts from the University of East Anglia (UEA) in Norwich and the Norfolk and Suffolk NHS Foundation Trust conducted a comprehensive review of the quality of information across major platforms.

The research team examined 27 distinct studies concerning 5,057 social media posts on platforms including YouTube, TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, and X. Dr. Alice Carter from UEA revealed that a staggering 52% of ADHD-related videos and 41% of autism videos on TikTok were classified as inaccurate.

The prevalence of misinformation was found to be consistently higher on TikTok than other platforms, though the platform has disputed these findings. The overall rating given for misinformation across various topics and platforms ranged from 0% for videos about anxiety and depression on YouTube Kids, to 56.9% for claustrophobia videos on YouTube.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The unchecked spread of these false narratives has severe real-world consequences for the healthcare system and the individuals involved. Dr. Eleanor Chatburn from UEA's Norwich Medical School highlighted that while questioning symptoms can be a helpful starting point, it must lead to proper clinical assessment with a professional rather than stopping at a social media algorithm.

Crucially, the study warns that this trend is leading to the misunderstanding of serious conditions and the pathologizing of ordinary human behavior. Furthermore, this wave of self-diagnosis based on false information can cause significant delays in actual diagnosis and treatment for people who genuinely require professional help.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The findings underscore a systemic failure in how social media algorithms prioritize and distribute health information. The authors of the study explicitly called out TikTok's algorithms for their tendency to push misinformation to vulnerable users, emphasizing a clear need for strengthened content moderation.

In stark contrast to platforms like TikTok, YouTube Kids was identified as the only platform to have zero misinformation on certain topics. The researchers attributed this success directly to the implementation of stricter content moderation and a deliberate prioritization of child-friendly, vetted content.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: The research team is demanding urgent action from social media companies to overhaul their content moderation strategies and prevent the further spread of medical misinformation. Judith Brown, head of evidence and research at the National Autistic Society, reinforced this, stating that companies must rethink how to improve their platforms to protect users.

Meanwhile, TikTok has pushed back against the findings, labeling the research a "flawed study" based on "outdated research about multiple platforms." A spokesperson claimed the platform removes harmful health misinformation and provides access to reliable information from the World Health Organization, setting the stage for a continued battle over digital accountability.
